timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — "The Customs of the Peoples Are Worthless"

heb.paragraph: Idols are hebel (v.3) — the same word Ecclesiastes uses for life's futility and Jeremiah 2:5 used for Israel becoming "worthless." The sustained idol polemic (vv.1–16) reads like an expansion of Isaiah 44:9–20, dismantling the manufacturing process step by step: a craftsman cuts a tree, decorates it with silver and gold, fastens it with nails — and then expects it to act. The satire is biting.

God is called "King of the nations" (v.7) — a title that transcends Israel's covenant exclusivity. If God is King of all nations, then idol worship is not merely Israel's sin but a universal folly. The title establishes God's sovereignty over the very nations whose gods Israel has been imitating.

refs.note: Isaiah's extended idol satire — the same craftsman who makes a god from half a log and cooks dinner with the other half.
"They have mouths but cannot speak, eyes but cannot see" — the psalm tradition of idol polemic that Jeremiah joins.
Paul at Athens: "We should not think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone — an image made by human design and skill."

notes.note: The step-by-step description of idol-making: cut a tree, shape it, overlay with silver and gold, fasten with nails. The idol is then "like a scarecrow in a cucumber field" — it cannot speak, cannot walk, cannot do harm or good.
"No one is like you, LORD" — the idol polemic serves as a foil for doxology. The inadequacy of idols magnifies the incomparability of God.

God is "the true God, the living God, the eternal King." His power is demonstrated in creation: thunder, rain, lightning. The manufactured idol versus the Creator God — the contrast could not be sharper.
Every goldsmith is "shamed by his idols." The images have no breath (rûaḥ) in them — they are the opposite of the living God who breathed life into humanity.
Calvin compares idols to scarecrows: "They stand in the field and terrify no birds. They stand in the temple and save no souls." The image captures the total impotence of manufactured religion.
Calvin emphasises the three titles: true (ʾĕmet), living (ḥayyîm), eternal (ʿôlām). Each contradicts a property of idols: idols are false, dead, and temporary.
The "scarecrow in a cucumber field" (kĕtōmer miqšâ) is literally a palm-trunk shaped like a human form — used to frighten birds. The comparison reduces temple statues to agricultural props.
This verse is in Aramaic, the only Aramaic verse in Jeremiah. It may be a response formula that exiles could use when confronted by Babylonian idol worship: "Tell them this..."

Lundbom notes that the idol polemic interrupts the judgment sequence (chs. 8–9, 11–12), functioning as a theological interlude that grounds the social critique in first-commandment theology: idolatry is the root sin that produces all other sins.

Brueggemann reads the idol polemic as "doxological resistance" — worship of the true God is itself an act of political defiance against the Babylonian empire and its claims to divine sponsorship. To mock Babylon's gods is to deny Babylon's authority.

hist.context: Chapter 10 inserts a hymnic celebration of God's sovereignty into the judgment oracles, creating a literary contrast. The idol polemic (vv.1–16) closely parallels Isaiah 40–44 and Psalm 115, forming part of a broader biblical tradition of satirizing manufactured gods. The chapter may have been addressed to exiles tempted by the impressive cult statues of Babylon — the idols that lined the processional way to Marduk's temple.

sections.header: Verses 17–25 — "Correct Me, LORD, but Only in Due Measure"

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah asks for correction "with justice" (bĕmišpāṭ), not "in anger" — discipline proportionate to the offense, not annihilating wrath. The word mûsār carries the dual sense of discipline and instruction (as in Proverbs). Jeremiah accepts that judgment is deserved but pleads for measured correction rather than destruction.

refs.note: "Do not despise the LORD's discipline" — the wisdom tradition that contextualizes Jeremiah's prayer for measured correction.
Habakkuk similarly asks: "In wrath remember mercy" — the prophetic plea for justice tempered by compassion.

notes.note: The tent metaphor: "My tent is destroyed, all my ropes are snapped." Shepherds (rulers) have failed, and the flock is scattered. The imagery anticipates exile.
"I know, LORD, that people's lives are not their own; it is not for them to direct their steps." This confession of human dependence undergirds the plea for proportionate judgment.

Calvin uses this verse as a proof-text for divine sovereignty over human affairs: "Man proposes but God disposes." If humans cannot even direct their own steps, how much less can they save themselves?

The phrase "it is not for a person to direct their steps" uses the verb kûn in the hiphil ("to establish/direct"). The theological claim is that human autonomy is an illusion — our paths are shaped by forces beyond our control.

Lundbom reads vv.23–25 as a communal lament adapted from psalm traditions — note the close parallel with Psalm 79:6–7. The prayer positions Judah between deserved judgment and the hope that God's wrath will fall more heavily on nations that do not know him.

Brueggemann identifies a tension between submission to divine sovereignty (v.23) and the plea for redirected wrath (v.25). The prayer is theologically honest rather than perfectly consistent — real prayer often holds contradictory impulses together.

hist.context: The chapter closes with a prayer that transitions from communal lament to personal petition. The "tent" imagery (vv.19–20) may reflect the coming exile — tents pulled up, cords snapped, children gone. The prayer in v.23–24, echoed in Psalm 6:1, acknowledges human inability to direct one's own path while appealing for tempered judgment.

rows.text: Idol Polemic: Scarecrows in a Field

rows.label: vv.6–10
rows.text: Doxology: No One Like the LORD

rows.label: vv.11–16
rows.text: The Aramaic Verdict and Creator Hymn

lit.note: Chapter 10 juxtaposes idol critique with doxology, then moves to communal lament. The Aramaic verse (v.11) is unique in Jeremiah — a ready-made response for exiles confronting Babylonian religion.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10 — God as incomparable Creator versus manufactured idols. Worship at 9 reflects the chapter's sustained polemic on true versus false worship.

hebtext.note: Idols are hebel (v.3) — the same word Ecclesiastes uses for life's futility and Jeremiah 2:5 used for Israel becoming "worthless." The sustained idol polemic (vv.1–16) reads like an expansion of Isaiah 44:9–20, dismantling the manufacturing process step by step: a craftsman cuts a tree, decorates it with silver and gold, fastens it with nails — and then expects it to act. The satire is biting.

God is called "King of the nations" (v.7) — a title that transcends Israel's covenant exclusivity. If God is King of all nations, then idol worship is not merely Israel's sin but a universal folly. The title establishes God's sovereignty over the very nations whose gods Israel has been imitating.

Jeremiah asks for correction "with justice" (bĕmišpāṭ), not "in anger" — discipline proportionate to the offense, not annihilating wrath. The word mûsār carries the dual sense of discipline and instruction (as in Proverbs). Jeremiah accepts that judgment is deserved but pleads for measured correction rather than destruction.

ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: This verse is in Aramaic, the only Aramaic verse in Jeremiah…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The LORD vs. Idols

translations.text: Hammered silver is brought from Tarshish and gold from Uphaz. What the craftsman and goldsmith have made is then dressed in blue and purple— all made by skilled workers.

Beaten silver is brought from Tarshish, and gold from Uphaz. They are the work of the craftsman and of the hands of the goldsmith; their clothing is violet and purple; they are all the work of skilled men.
Like a scarecrow in a cucumber field, their idols cannot speak; they must be carried because they cannot walk. Do not fear them; they can do no harm nor can they do any good.”

Their idols are like scarecrows in a cucumber field, and they cannot speak; they have to be carried, for they cannot walk. Do not be afraid of them, for they cannot do evil, neither is it in them to do good.”

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "The step-by-step description of idol-making: cut a tree, shape it, overlay with silver and gold, fasten with nails. The idol is then "like a scarecrow in a cucumber field" — it cannot speak, cannot wa…"

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin compares idols to scarecrows: "They stand in the field and terrify no birds. They stand in the temple and save no souls." The image captures the total impotence of manufactured religion."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The "scarecrow in a cucumber field" (kĕtōmer miqšâ) is literally a palm-trunk shaped like a human form — used to frighten birds. The comparison reduces temple statues to agricultural props."

thread.text: Isaiah's extended idol satire — the same craftsman who makes a god from half a log and cooks dinner with the other half.
"They have mouths but cannot speak, eyes but cannot see" — the psalm tradition of idol polemic that Jeremiah joins.
Paul at Athens: "We should not think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone — an image made by human design and skill."
"Do not despise the LORD's discipline" — the wisdom tradition that contextualizes Jeremiah's prayer for measured correction.
Habakkuk similarly asks: "In wrath remember mercy" — the prophetic plea for justice tempered by compassion.

coaching.tip: The idol satire (vv. 3–5) — cut a tree, decorate it, nail it down so it won't topple — reads like a description of a Christmas tree, which is why some commentators have noted the parallel. But Jeremiah's point is broader: manufactured gods require maintenance by their makers. The creature sustains the creator. The relationship runs backward, and that's the diagnostic for idolatry in any era.

'Correct me, LORD, but only in due measure — not in your anger, or you will reduce me to nothing' (v. 24). Jeremiah asks for proportional discipline rather than annihilating wrath. He doesn't claim innocence; he pleads for mercy within judgment. The prayer assumes a God who can modulate — who is capable of measured correction. Even in judgment, Jeremiah expects divine restraint.
